author: Tertullian
book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
Sundry Passages in the Great Chapter of the Resurrection of the Dead Explained in Defence of Our Doctrine.

ccel.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

title: Sundry Passages in the Great Chapter of the Resurrection of the Dead Explained in Defence of Our Doctrine.

source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But “flesh and blood,” you say, “cannot inherit the kingdom of God.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: We are quite aware that this too is written; but although our opponents place it in the front of the battle, we have intentionally reserved the objection until now, in order that we may in our last assault overthrow it, after we have removed out of the way all the questions which are auxiliary to it.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: However, they must contrive to recall to their mind even now our preceding arguments, in order that the occasion which originally suggested this passage may assist our judgment in arriving at its meaning.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: The apostle, as I take it, having set forth for the Corinthians the details of their church discipline, had summed up the substance of his own gospel, and of their belief in an exposition of the Lord’s death and resurrection, for the purpose of deducing therefrom the rule of our hope, and the groundwork thereof.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Accordingly he subjoins this statement: “Now if Christ be preached that He rose from the dead, how say some among you that there is no resurrection of the dead?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: If there be no resurrection of the dead, then Christ is not risen: and if Christ be not risen, then is our preaching vain, and your faith is also vain.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Yea, and we are found false witnesses of God; because we have testified of God that He raised up Christ, whom He raised not up, if so be that the dead rise not.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For if the dead rise not, then is not Christ raised: and if Christ be not raised, your faith is vain, because ye are yet in your sins, and they which have fallen asleep in Christ are perished.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now, what is the point which he evidently labours hard to make us believe throughout this passage?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: The resurrection of the dead, you say, which was denied: he certainly wished it to be believed on the strength of the example which he adduced—the Lord’s resurrection.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Certainly, you say.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Well now, is an example borrowed from different circumstances, or from like ones?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: From like ones, by all means, is your answer.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: How then did Christ rise again?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: In the flesh, or not?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: No doubt, since you are told that He “died according to the Scriptures,” and “that He was buried according to the Scriptures,” no otherwise than in the flesh, you will also allow that it was in the flesh that He was raised from the dead.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For the very same body which fell in death, and which lay in the sepulchre, did also rise again; (and it was) not so much Christ in the flesh, as the flesh in Christ.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: If, therefore, we are to rise again after the example of Christ, who rose in the flesh, we shall certainly not rise according to that example, unless we also shall ourselves rise again in the flesh.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: “For,” he says, “since by man came death, by man came also the resurrection of the dead.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: (This he says) in order, on the one hand, to distinguish the two authors—Adam of death, Christ of resurrection; and, on the other hand, to make the resurrection operate on the same substance as the death, by comparing the authors themselves under the designation man.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For if “as in Adam all die, even so in Christ shall all be made alive,” their vivification in Christ must be in the flesh, since it is in the flesh that arises their death in Adam.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: “But every man in his own order,” because of course it will be also every man in his own body.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For the order will be arranged severally, on account of the individual merits.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now, as the merits must be ascribed to the body, it must needs follow that the order also should be arranged in respect of the bodies, that it may be in relation to their merits.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But inasmuch as “some are also baptized for the dead,” we will see whether there be a good reason for this.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now it is certain that they adopted this (practice) with such a presumption as made them suppose that the vicarious baptism (in question) would be beneficial to the flesh of another in anticipation of the resurrection; for unless it were a bodily resurrection, there would be no pledge secured by this process of a corporeal baptism.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: “Why are they then baptized for the dead,” he asks, unless the bodies rise again which are thus baptized?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For it is not the soul which is sanctified by the baptismal bath: its sanctification comes from the “answer.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: “And why,” he inquires, “stand we in jeopardy every hour?”—meaning, of course, through the flesh.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: “I die daily,” (says he); that is, undoubtedly, in the perils of the body, in which “he even fought with beasts at Ephesus,”—even with those beasts which caused him such peril and trouble in Asia, to which he alludes in his second epistle to the same church of Corinth: “For we would not, brethren, have you ignorant of our trouble which came to us in Asia, that we were pressed above measure, above strength, insomuch that we despaired even of life.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now, if I mistake not, he enumerates all these particulars in order that in his unwillingness to have his conflicts in the flesh supposed to be useless, he may induce an unfaltering belief in the resurrection of the flesh.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: For useless must that conflict be deemed (which is sustained in a body) for which no resurrection is in prospect.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: “But some man will say, How are the dead to be raised?

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: And with what body will they come?”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now here he discusses the qualities of bodies, whether it be the very same, or different ones, which men are to resume.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Since, however, such a question as this must be regarded as a subsequent one, it will in passing be enough for us that the resurrection is determined to be a bodily one even from this, that it is about the quality of bodies that the inquiry arises.
